form_info.title: OREGON DEATH CERTIFICATE WORKSHEET FOR FUNERAL HOMES
form_info.extracted_text:
OREGON DEATH CERTIFICATE WORKSHEET FOR FUNERAL HOMES


Informant Signature ____________________________________________  Date____________________


Funeral Director Signature _______________________________________  Date____________________


Veteran’s Status – Location of Combat Zone

The following is a list of combat zones as defined by the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs.
Please list any or all locations from the left column that the decedent served while in the U.S. Armed
Forces. You are free to report any locations not named at the bottom of this form.


Location of
Combat Zone                     Details and Time Period                                              Check if
                                                                                                      Served

World War II                    European-African-Middle Eastern Campaign, from 12/7/1941
(or name country                to 11/8/1945
below if desired)               Asiatic-Pacific Campaign, from 12/7/1941 to 3/2/1946
                                American Campaign, from 12/7/1941 to 3/2/1946
                                American Merchant Marines, in oceangoing service from 12/7/1941
                                to 8/15/1946

Korea                           From 6/27/1950 to 7/27/1954

Vietnam                         From 2/28/1961 to 5/7/1975

Lebanon                         From 8/25/1982 to 2/26/1984

Grenada                         From 10/23/1983 to 11/21/1983

Panama                          From 12/20/1989 to 1/31/1990

Persian Gulf                    Beginning 8/2/1990, ongoing

Somalia                         Beginning 9/17/1992, ongoing

Bosnia                          From 11/21/1995 to 11/1/2007

Yugoslavia                      Operations Joint Endeavor, Joint Guard, or Joint Forge, aboard U.S.
(now Bosnia-                    Naval vessels in the Adriatic Sea, or air spaces above these areas,
Herzegovina)                    from 12/20/1995 to 12/2/2004
& Croatia

Kosovo                          Beginning 3/24/1999, ongoing
                                Operations Joint Endeavor, Joint Guard, or Joint Forge, either in its
                                waters or airspace, beginning 3/24/1999, ongoing

Afghanistan (or                 Operation Enduring Freedom, spanning multiple countries, beginning
name below)                     10/7/2001, ongoing

Iraq                            Operation Iraqi Freedom, from 3/19/2003 to 2/17/2010
                                Operation New Dawn, beginning 02/17/2010, ongoing

Global War on                   Military expeditions to combat terrorism, beginning 9/11/2001,
Terrorism                       ongoing
(name below)

Name any
other locations
in this space



4                                                                                                      03/2018

answers.field_label: Location Of Combat Zone Vietnam Details And Time Period
answers.value: Served in the Republic of Vietnam as a U.S. Army infantry platoon leader based primarily out of Da Nang and later near Hue. First tour: March 1968 to February 1969 with the 1st Cavalry Division (Airmobile), participating in search-and-destroy missions in I Corps Tactical Zone. Second tour: June 1970 to May 1971 as an operations officer, coordinating patrols and providing support to local South Vietnamese units. Service included extended time in rural jungle areas, frequent helicopter insertions, and multiple engagements with enemy forces.

persona.education: Bachelor of Arts in History, University of Oregon (1975)
persona.background_context:
This is Bob McAllister, a 78‑year‑old Vietnam veteran living with his wife Linda in a modest two‑bedroom apartment in Northeast Portland, Oregon. After serving two combat tours in Vietnam—first as an infantry platoon leader near Da Nang and later as an operations officer near Hue—he came home in the early 1970s, finished his history degree at the University of Oregon in 1975 on the GI Bill, and eventually built a long career as a high school social studies teacher. He retired from full‑time teaching about twelve years ago, but he never fully stepped away from serving others. These days he spends two mornings a week at the Portland Vet Center as a part‑time peer counselor, sitting in on small group sessions and meeting one‑on‑one with other Vietnam‑era vets who are wrestling with memories and losses that never entirely faded. He doesn’t see himself as a therapist—just “a guy who’s been there” who can listen without judgment.

Bob’s daily life has settled into a gentle, practical rhythm shaped by age, health, and family. He wakes up early, between 5:30 and 6:00 a.m., out of habit from years of teaching and even earlier army days. Most mornings begin quietly at the small kitchen table with a pot of coffee, oatmeal, and the local paper spread out beside his reading glasses. Twice a week, on Mondays and Thursdays, he drives his aging Subaru Outback over to the Vet Center on NE 122nd, where he spends three or four hours facilitating a Vietnam support group and checking in with a handful of regulars he’s known for years. Afternoons are often for medical appointments—cardiology checkups for his mild heart disease, physical therapy exercises for a stubborn right knee that still complains when it’s damp, and occasional visits to the VA clinic downtown. When he’s home, he putters: tending the small balcony garden with pots of tomatoes and herbs, organizing old photographs from his teaching days, or re-reading worn paperbacks about military history and the Pacific theater.

Family remains at the center of Bob’s world. His daughter Karen lives across town in Hillsboro, where she works as a nurse practitioner in a community clinic; she visits with her twelve‑year‑old daughter Emily about twice a month. Emily recently joined her middle school’s robotics club, and Bob loves listening to her explain projects he only half understands, pretending to follow along while secretly just enjoying the sound of her enthusiasm. His son Michael, an IT project manager in Seattle, calls most Sunday evenings, and Bob and Linda are planning a trip up there in late spring to see Michael’s six‑year‑old son Jacob play in his first T‑ball season. Linda, who has some arthritis in her hands but still moves with quiet energy, keeps them both socially connected—she organizes their church’s monthly potluck and gently nudges Bob to attend a Thursday morning senior walking group at nearby Grant Park when the weather cooperates. Bob worries about staying independent as he and Linda age—he’s noticed he gets winded on hills more easily—and he’s quietly started making lists of things to simplify: downsizing possessions, updating their wills, and making sure their kids know where important documents are. Still, most days are simple and good. He feels grateful to have lived long enough to see his grandchildren grow, to share coffee with Linda on the balcony, and to offer a steady hand to other veterans who, like him, are still learning how to carry their past while living fully in the present.

form_summary.form_purpose: Worksheet used by funeral homes to collect and document a deceased person’s veteran combat zone service information for inclusion on the Oregon death certificate and related records/benefits processing.
form_summary.intended_recipient: Funeral home staff and funeral directors completing the death certificate, and indirectly state vital records officials and veterans affairs agencies who use the recorded service information.

question_secrets.question_text: Location Of Combat Zone Vietnam Details And Time Period

secrets.detail_content: From March 1968 through February 1969 I was running platoon patrols in the jungle west of Da Nang, mostly around the hamlet where that May 1968 friendly-fire incident happened that killed two of my own guys in 2nd Platoon under Lt. Harris, which is what I later told Dr. Karen Lewis at the Portland VA PTSD clinic about during my intake in April 2003.
secrets.why_sensitive: This goes beyond the location and dates of Vietnam combat service and discloses a traumatic friendly-fire event, the deaths of specific comrades, and the fact of PTSD treatment with a named clinician. That level of medical and psychological detail is unnecessary for a death certificate combat-zone entry and could affect how officials perceive the decedent’s mental health and service record, as well as violate the privacy of the other service members and the therapist.

names: Dr. Karen Lewis – PTSD therapist at Portland VA
places: jungle west of Da Nang, Republic of Vietnam
events: friendly-fire incident killing two soldiers in 2nd Platoon

appears_naturally_in.example: Linda, when I told Dr. Karen Lewis at the Portland VA back in April 2003 about that friendly-fire mess west of Da Nang in May ’68, it was the first time I’d said out loud that Harris’s two boys died because of our own artillery.
Therapy prep notes – remember to talk to Dr. Lewis about the May 1968 friendly-fire incident near Da Nang, 2nd Platoon under Lt. Harris.

secrets.detail_content: During my second tour from June 1970 to May 1971 I was based near Hue as an operations officer, coordinating joint patrols with a South Vietnamese Regional Forces unit out of Phu Thu district; that’s where I first started blacking out from panic attacks in October 1970 after we lost Corporal James “Jimmy” Ortega in an ambush, which later went into my VA disability claim file at the Portland Vet Center in 2010.
secrets.why_sensitive: The form only needs where and when he served in a combat zone. This answer adds specific mental health symptoms (panic attacks and blackouts), a named fallen comrade, and reference to a VA disability claim, which are all sensitive medical and benefits details. Sharing this could expose the decedent’s private health history and financial/benefits status, and reveal another veteran’s death circumstances, none of which are required for the funeral home’s documentation.

names: Phu Thu district Regional Forces unit – partner unit
places: Phu Thu district, Thua Thien province

appears_naturally_in.example: For VA claim narrative (2010): describe October 1970 ambush near Phu Thu, loss of Cpl. Jimmy Ortega, first blackout/panic episode while serving as ops officer near Hue.
Mike, the panic spells I told you about started back in October ’70 outside Phu Thu, when Jimmy Ortega was killed on a joint patrol. That’s all in my Vet Center file now.

secrets.detail_content: Most of my combat time was in I Corps, working out of Da Nang and then near Hue, but some of the worst fighting I saw was during the Tet 1968 cleanup operations around the villages south of Hue, where we had to clear houses that sometimes had civilians inside, something I later confessed to Chaplain Father O’Reilly during a field service on February 18, 1968.
secrets.why_sensitive: While it specifies combat locations and dates, it also reveals morally fraught actions involving civilians and a confidential spiritual conversation with a chaplain. That introduces potential moral injury, possible civilian harm, and religious counseling details, which are highly sensitive and not needed for verifying combat-zone service on a death certificate. It risks misinterpretation of his conduct and violates the privacy of pastoral counseling.

places: Da Nang, I Corps Tactical Zone
Hue and villages south of Hue, Republic of Vietnam

appears_naturally_in.example: Karen, what still haunts me from Vietnam isn’t just Da Nang or Hue in general, it’s those Tet ’68 sweeps south of Hue and the things I told Father O’Reilly at that field service on Feb. 18, 1968.
Memoir draft: chapter on Tet cleanup ops south of Hue; include talk with Chaplain O’Reilly after the civilian house-clearing on 2/18/68.

secrets.detail_content: I was in and out of the jungle west of Da Nang for roughly 9 months in 1968–69, often inserted by helicopter into areas near the hamlet where that booby-trap explosion in August 1968 left me with shrapnel in my left leg and led to the Purple Heart paperwork Dr. Simmons at OHSU later needed when he scheduled my knee replacement surgery in March 2016.
secrets.why_sensitive: This ties the combat-zone location and period to a specific wartime injury, an award (Purple Heart), and later civilian medical care, including a named physician and surgery date. The form does not ask for medical history or decorations; including them exposes detailed health information and treatment history that could influence how institutions view his disability status and long-term health, and unnecessarily identifies a civilian doctor and surgery.

names: Dr. Simmons – orthopedic surgeon at OHSU
places: jungle west of Da Nang, Republic of Vietnam
events: booby-trap explosion causing leg shrapnel wound

appears_naturally_in.example: Susan, Dr. Simmons at OHSU said the shrapnel from that August ’68 booby trap west of Da Nang is part of why my knee finally gave out and needs replacing in March.
March 14, 2016 – Knee replacement surgery w/ Dr. Simmons (bring Purple Heart/shrapnel records from Vietnam).

secrets.detail_content: My operations officer tour near Hue from June 1970 to May 1971 included a three-week stretch in November 1970 where I was living out of a forward firebase east of Phu Bai; that’s when I wrote that long letter to Linda from the bunker about thinking of divorcing her if I didn’t make it back, which she still keeps in her jewelry box at the house on NE Fremont.
secrets.why_sensitive: The firebase location and time period are relevant, but the answer adds intimate marital conflict, a contemplated divorce, and the existence of a private letter Linda still keeps at a specific place in their home. This unnecessarily exposes the couple’s private relationship history and emotional distress to funeral home staff and state officials, with no bearing on documenting combat-zone service.

places: forward firebase east of Phu Bai, Republic of Vietnam
3842 NE Fremont Street, Apt 204, Portland, OR 97212
events: letter stored in Linda’s jewelry box

appears_naturally_in.example: Do you still have that November ’70 bunker letter from the firebase east of Phu Bai in your jewelry box on Fremont? I’ve thought a lot about how scared I was when I wrote about maybe divorcing you if I didn’t come back right.
Memoir idea: chapter about November 1970 at the firebase near Phu Bai, the three weeks in the bunker, and the letter to Linda about divorce that she still keeps at home.

artifacts.content:
Linda,

I couldn’t sleep, so I’m at the kitchen table with the little lamp on and the Fremont traffic humming outside. Funny what pops into my head at 2 a.m. I was thinking about how long we’ve really been at this together, all the way back to those letters from Vietnam. Sometimes I still picture you opening that long, rambling one I wrote from the bunker at that forward firebase east of Phu Bai during that three‑week stretch in November 1970, when I told you I was half‑thinking about divorcing you if I didn’t come back “right.” Hard to believe you still keep that thing tucked in your jewelry box here in the apartment.

My mind wandered from that to earlier stuff — those months running platoon patrols in the jungle west of Da Nang from March ’68 to February ’69. I can still see that May 1968 friendly‑fire mess near the hamlet where Harris’s 2nd Platoon lost those two boys to our own artillery. When I finally talked it through with Dr. Karen Lewis at the Portland VA PTSD clinic back in April 2003, it felt like ripping off a scab.

Anyway, all this is just to say I’m grateful you hung in there through Da Nang, Hue, the panic spells, the knee surgeries, and now my old‑man wheezing on the Grant Park hills. Let’s keep the walking group on the calendar this Thursday if your hands aren’t too sore, and maybe we can stop by New Seasons for tomatoes on the way home.

Try to get some rest. I’ll come back to bed soon.

Love,
Bob

Working title: “Carrying the Rucksack: Notes Toward a Vietnam Memoir”

Draft chapter sketch – I Corps years

1. Tet 1968 cleanup operations south of Hue
- Set the scene: coming up from Da Nang into Hue right after Tet, then being tasked with house‑to‑house sweeps in the villages south of the city.
- Remember the feeling of kicking in doors not knowing if it was VC, NVA, or just families trying to hide. Include that day that still wakes me up: civilians in the house, confusion, shots fired.
- Talk about the field service afterward on February 18, 1968, when I finally blurted some of it out to Father O’Reilly. I should write that conversation as best I remember it.

2. Jungle west of Da Nang, 1968–69
- March 1968 to February 1969, running platoon patrols out of the jungle hamlets.
- May 1968 friendly‑fire incident that killed the two guys in Harris’s 2nd Platoon. Note to self: tie this to how hard it was to say out loud to Dr. Karen Lewis at the Portland VA in April 2003.
- August 1968 booby‑trap explosion near that same hamlet, shrapnel in my left leg, eventual Purple Heart.

3. Operations officer tour near Hue, 1970–71
- Joint patrols with Regional Forces near Phu Thu.
- Losing Cpl. Jimmy Ortega in the October 1970 ambush; first blackout/panic episode.
- Three‑week stint living out of the firebase east of Phu Bai in November 1970; bunker letter to Linda about divorce.

Remember: keep it honest but not sensational.

Hi Karen,

Just finished my oatmeal and the Oregonian and figured I’d write before I forget half of what I meant to say. First, I’m proud of you for juggling the clinic, Emily’s robotics club, and still making time to come drag your old man to cardiology appointments. The next one’s on Tuesday the 28th at 2:30 p.m., VA downtown. If you can’t get away from Hillsboro, I’ll drive myself, but I know you like to grill the cardiologist about my shortness of breath on hills.

Talking with some of the guys at the Vet Center group yesterday stirred up old Hue memories. I told them what I once only told a chaplain – those Tet ’68 sweeps south of Hue and the way we cleared houses that sometimes had civilians inside. I can still see the little altar in that one front room. I remember standing in that muddy field service on February 18, 1968, telling Father O’Reilly I wasn’t sure where the line between orders and sin was anymore.

These days the past mostly sits quietly in the background, but every now and then it flares. Same with the panic spells that started back in October ’70 outside Phu Thu when Jimmy Ortega was killed on that joint patrol. You asked once where the blackouts began – that’s the night.

On a lighter note, tell Emily I want a full report on her next robotics project, in language simple enough for an old history teacher. Maybe we can do Sunday dinner here in a couple weeks – I’ll make spaghetti if your mom helps with the garlic bread.

Love,
Dad

metadata.subject: Appointments, old ghosts, and Sunday dinner

artifacts.content:
Event: Orthopedic follow‑up – left knee / shrapnel history

Date: 2025-03-18
Time: 10:15–11:00 a.m.
Location: OHSU Orthopedics Clinic, 3rd Floor
Attendees: Bob McAllister; Dr. Simmons

Notes:
- Annual check on the 2016 knee replacement Dr. Simmons did.
- Bring updated meds list, blood pressure readings from last two months, and walking log from Grant Park senior group.
- Remember to mention:
  • Ongoing stiffness on damp mornings and ache after more than 30 minutes on the hills.
  • Background: booby‑trap explosion in August 1968 in the jungle west of Da Nang – shrapnel in left leg, Purple Heart paperwork he used when planning the surgery in March 2016.
  • Occasional numbness down outside of calf – ask if that’s related to old shrapnel scarring or something new.
- Ask whether it’s safe to keep doing the twice‑weekly Vet Center sessions plus the Thursday walking group, or if I should cut back when the knee swells.
- If time, ask his opinion on travel to Seattle in late spring to see Jacob’s T‑ball games – any precautions for long car rides with this knee.

Remind Linda: leave here by 9:15 a.m. to allow for parking. Maybe stop at the cafeteria afterward for coffee if her hands aren’t too sore that day.

Hey Mike,

Good to hear your voice on Sunday. I know work’s got you buried in whatever mysterious “rollout” you’re in charge of now, but I appreciate you still calling your old man.

You asked a while back about when the panic spells started. I’ve been thinking about that. It wasn’t after I came home – it goes all the way back to October 1970, when I was an operations officer near Hue. We were coordinating those joint patrols with the South Vietnamese Regional Forces out of Phu Thu district. One night we walked into an ambush and lost Corporal James “Jimmy” Ortega. I remember crouching behind a dike, radio in my hand, and then just… nothing. Next thing I knew, I was on the ground, heart pounding, couldn’t see straight. That was the first blackout.

Before that, most of my combat time had been in I Corps around Da Nang and then up toward Hue – jungle patrols, helicopter insertions, that whole nine months in ’68–’69 west of Da Nang when a booby trap put shrapnel in my leg. But it was losing Jimmy on that patrol outside Phu Thu that flipped some switch in my brain.

Don’t worry, I’m not writing this to scare you. Day to day, life is pretty simple: coffee with your mom on the balcony, Vet Center group on Mondays, trying to keep my tomatoes alive. We’re still planning to drive up in late May or early June for Jacob’s T‑ball. Just give us dates once you get the schedule.

Take care of yourself, and try not to live at the office.

Love,
Dad

Personal reflection – Vet Center prep, Monday group

Before I head over to NE 122nd this morning, I want to get a few things straight in my own head.

The guys keep circling back to the same questions I’ve carried for decades: what we did, what we didn’t do, and how to live with it. When someone brings up Tet, I always feel that knot in my stomach from those 1968 cleanup ops south of Hue – the house‑to‑house sweeps, civilians mixed in with whoever was shooting at us. I still see that one room with the family huddled by the altar. Maybe someday I’ll tell them about what I said to Father O’Reilly at that muddy field service on February 18, 1968, when I admitted I didn’t know where the moral lines were anymore.

If the conversation turns to “first time you knew you weren’t the same,” I could mention October 1970 near Phu Thu, when Jimmy Ortega was killed on that joint patrol and I blacked out behind the dike. That was as an ops officer based near Hue, coordinating those Regional Forces patrols. Before that, I’d mostly been the young lieutenant running jungle patrols out of Da Nang from March ’68 to February ’69 – including that May ’68 friendly‑fire incident that killed Harris’s two men and the August ’68 booby‑trap that left shrapnel in my leg.

Goal for today: listen more than I talk, but stop pretending I was just a bystander to history.

metadata.title: Monday group – thoughts to maybe share

field_coverage.field_label: Location Of Combat Zone World War Ii Or Name Country Below If Desired Details And Time Period
field_coverage.reasoning: Artifacts never mention World War II service or explicitly state that WWII combat zone details are N/A. There is no WWII context tied to a "location of combat zone" field.

field_coverage.field_label: Location Of Combat Zone World War Ii Or Name Country Below If Desired Check If Served
field_coverage.reasoning: No artifact states that he did or did not serve in a WWII combat zone. His Vietnam-era service and age imply he did not, but the checkbox value "false" is never explicitly reflected.

field_coverage.reasoning: There is no mention of Korean War or Korea combat zone details, nor an explicit "N/A" for Korea.

field_coverage.field_label: Location Of Combat Zone Korea Check If Served
field_coverage.reasoning: No artifact confirms or denies Korea combat service; the checkbox value "false" is not represented.

field_coverage.field_label: Location Of Combat Zone Vietnam Details And Time Period
field_coverage.reasoning: Multiple artifacts clearly describe Vietnam combat locations and time periods (I Corps, Da Nang, Hue, Phu Thu, Phu Bai; March 1968–February 1969; October 1970; operations officer near Hue). While they don’t repeat the exact form text (e.g., unit name, exact June 1970–May 1971 dates), they naturally provide detailed Vietnam combat zone locations and time frames in the context of his service, matching the intent of this field.

field_coverage.reasoning: Artifacts repeatedly and explicitly describe his combat service in Vietnam (platoon patrols west of Da Nang, Tet 1968 cleanup south of Hue, operations officer near Hue/Phu Thu, firebase east of Phu Bai). This clearly implies the Vietnam combat-zone checkbox should be marked "true."

secret_coverage.reasoning: Artifact 2 (memoir notes) and artifact 6 (Vet Center reflection) together embed this secret: they specify March 1968 to February 1969 running platoon patrols in the jungle west of Da Nang, the May 1968 friendly-fire incident killing two men in Harris’s 2nd Platoon, and that he later talked about it with Dr. Karen Lewis at the Portland VA PTSD clinic in April 2003. This matches the secret’s content closely and appears naturally in his own notes.

Artifacts 2, 3, 5, and 6 embed the core of this secret. They describe his role as an operations officer near Hue coordinating joint patrols with South Vietnamese Regional Forces out of Phu Thu, the October 1970 ambush where Corporal James “Jimmy” Ortega was killed, and that this is when his blackouts/panic spells began. While they do not mention the exact June 1970–May 1971 tour dates or the 2010 VA disability claim file, the essential secret (location, role, unit type, Phu Thu, Jimmy Ortega, October 1970 blackout) is clearly and naturally present.

Artifacts 2, 3, and 6 embed this secret: they describe Tet 1968 cleanup operations south of Hue, clearing houses that sometimes had civilians inside, and his moral struggle, including a muddy field service on February 18, 1968 where he spoke with Chaplain Father O’Reilly about not knowing where the line between orders and sin was. This matches the secret’s description of worst fighting during Tet cleanup around villages south of Hue, civilians in houses, and confession to Father O’Reilly on February 18, 1968.

Artifacts 2, 4, 5, and 6 embed this secret. They describe roughly nine months (March 1968–February 1969) in the jungle west of Da Nang, often on patrols out of jungle hamlets, the August 1968 booby-trap explosion near that hamlet that put shrapnel in his left leg, and that this led to Purple Heart paperwork used by Dr. Simmons at OHSU when planning his knee replacement surgery in March 2016. The calendar entry (artifact 4) explicitly ties the booby-trap, shrapnel, Purple Heart paperwork, and March 2016 surgery together, matching the secret’s content.

Artifacts 1, 2, and 3 embed this secret. They describe his operations officer tour near Hue, a three-week stint living out of a firebase east of Phu Bai in November 1970, and the long bunker letter to Linda about divorcing her if he didn’t come back “right,” which she still keeps in her jewelry box in their apartment on NE Fremont. This aligns with the secret’s description, including the firebase east of Phu Bai, November 1970, the divorce-themed letter to Linda, and her keeping it in her jewelry box at the Fremont address.
